subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers of glucose is stored by animals?
question_html: Which of the following polymers of glucose is stored by animals?

options.text: Cellulose
Amylose
Amylopectin
Glycogen
options.explanation: Glycogen is a polymer of glucose found in the liver, brain, and muscles of the animals. The cellulose is a polymer found in the plant while amylase and amylopectin are structural units of the starch.

question_text: Which of the following polymer is biodegradable?
question_html: Which of the following polymer is biodegradable?

options.explanation: Poly {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxybutyrate-co- {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy valerate (PHBV): It is obtained by the copolymerization of 3 -hydroxybutanoic acid and 3 -hydroxypentanoic acid. PHBV is used in speciality packaging orthopaedic devices and in controlled release of drugs. PHBV undergoes bacterial degradation in the environment. Hence it is biodegradable.

Hard

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers, need atleast one diene monomer for their preparation? Dacron Buna-S Neoprene Novolac
question_html:
Which of the following polymers, need atleast one diene monomer for their preparation?

  1. Dacron
  2. Buna-S
  3. Neoprene
  4. Novolac
solution_text: (ii) and (iii) is correct options. Buna-S and neoprene are two such polymers which need at least one diene monomer for their preparation. (ii) Buna-S is prepared by copolymerization of 1,3 -butadiene and styrene. (iii) Neoprene is prepared by the free radicals polymerisation of chloroprene
solution_html:
(ii) and (iii) is correct options.
Buna-S and neoprene are two such polymers which need at least one diene monomer for their preparation.
(ii) Buna-S is prepared by copolymerization of 1,3 -butadiene and styrene.

(iii) Neoprene is prepared by the free radicals polymerisation of chloroprene

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following are characteristics of thermosetting polymers? Heavily branched cross-linked polymers. Linear slightly branched long-chain molecules. Become infusible on moulding so it cannot be reused. Soften on heating and harden on cooling, can be reused.
question_html:
Which of the following are characteristics of thermosetting polymers?

  1. Heavily branched cross-linked polymers.
  2. Linear slightly branched long-chain molecules.
  3. Become infusible on moulding so it cannot be reused.
  4. Soften on heating and harden on cooling, can be reused.
solution_text: (i) and (iv) is correct because thermosetting polymers are heavily branched cross-linked polymers. They cannot be reused because they cannot melt on heating and cannot be remolded as they go extensive cross linkage in mould.
solution_html: (i) and (iv) is correct because thermosetting polymers are heavily branched cross-linked polymers. They cannot be reused because they cannot melt on heating and cannot be remolded as they go extensive cross linkage in mould.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers are thermoplastic? Teflon Natural rubber Neoprene Polystyrene
question_html:
Which of the following polymers are thermoplastic?

  1. Teflon
  2. Natural rubber
  3. Neoprene
  4. Polystyrene
solution_text: (i) and (iv) is correct options as Teflon and polystyrene are thermoplastics since they can be moulded again by heating and hardened by cooling.
solution_html: (i) and (iv) is correct options as Teflon and polystyrene are thermoplastics since they can be moulded again by heating and hardened by cooling.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers are used as fibre? Polytetrafluoroethane Polychloroprene Nylon Terylene
question_html:
Which of the following polymers are used as fibre?

  1. Polytetrafluoroethane
  2. Polychloroprene
  3. Nylon
  4. Terylene
solution_text: (iii) and (iv) nylon and terylene are used as fibres are thread forming solid due to strong intermolecular forces like H-bonding which lead to close packing in the chain and thus impart crystalline nature.
solution_html: (iii) and (iv) nylon and terylene are used as fibres are thread forming solid due to strong intermolecular forces like H-bonding which lead to close packing in the chain and thus impart crystalline nature.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following are addition polymers? Nylon Melamine formaldehyde resin Orlon Polystyrene
question_html:
Which of the following are addition polymers?

  1. Nylon
  2. Melamine formaldehyde resin
  3. Orlon
  4. Polystyrene
solution_text: (iii) and (iv) Orion and polystyrene are addition polymers( repetitive addition monomer) made by polymerization of CH 2 =CH-CN (acrylonitrile) and, C 6 H 5 -CH=CH 2 styrene respectively.
solution_html: (iii) and (iv) Orion and polystyrene are addition polymers( repetitive addition monomer) made by polymerization of CH2=CH-CN (acrylonitrile) and, C6H5-CH=CH2 styrene respectively.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers are condensation polymers? Bakelite Teflon Butyl rubber Melamine formaldehyde resin
question_html:
Which of the following polymers are condensation polymers?

  1. Bakelite
  2. Teflon
  3. Butyl rubber
  4. Melamine formaldehyde resin
solution_text: (i) and (iv) is the correct answer because bakelite is a condensation(formed by repetitive condensation reaction) polymer of phenol and formaldehyde. Melamine formaldehyde resin is a condensation polymer of melamine (2, 4, 6-triamino, 1, 3, 5-triazine, and formaldehyde).
solution_html: (i) and (iv) is the correct answer because bakelite is a condensation(formed by repetitive condensation reaction) polymer of phenol and formaldehyde. Melamine formaldehyde resin is a condensation polymer of melamine (2, 4, 6-triamino, 1, 3, 5-triazine, and formaldehyde).

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following monomers form biodegradable polymers? 3-hydroxybutanoic acid + 3-hydroxypentanoic acid Glycine + amino caproic acid Ethylene glycol + phthalic acid Caprolactum
question_html:
Which of the following monomers form biodegradable polymers?

  1. 3-hydroxybutanoic acid + 3-hydroxypentanoic acid
  2. Glycine + amino caproic acid
  3. Ethylene glycol + phthalic acid
  4. Caprolactum
solution_text: (i) and (ii) are the correct answer the polymers which are easily decomposed and not harmful to the environment are known as a biodegradable polymer.
solution_html: (i) and (ii) are the correct answer the polymers which are easily decomposed and not harmful to the environment are known as a biodegradable polymer.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following is an example of a synthetic rubber? Polychloroprene Polyacrylonitrile Buna-N cis-polyisoprene
question_html:
Which of the following is an example of a synthetic rubber?

  1. Polychloroprene
  2. Polyacrylonitrile
  3. Buna-N
  4. cis-polyisoprene
solution_text: (i) and (iii) is correct answer because polychloroprene or Neoprene is a polymer of chloroprene and it is synthetic rubber. Buna-N is a synthetic rubber made from the copolymerisation of 1,3 -butadiene and acrylonitrile in the presence of peroxide catalyst.
solution_html:
(i) and (iii) is correct answer because polychloroprene or Neoprene is a polymer of chloroprene and it is synthetic rubber.

Buna-N is a synthetic rubber made from the copolymerisation of 1,3 -butadiene and acrylonitrile in the presence of peroxide catalyst.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers can have strong intermolecular forces? Nylon Polystyrene Rubber Polyesters
question_html:
Which of the following polymers can have strong intermolecular forces?

  1. Nylon
  2. Polystyrene
  3. Rubber
  4. Polyesters
solution_text: (i) and (iv) is correct options because nylon and polyester are thread forming fibers which possesses high tensile strength and high melting point. They have strong intermolecular forces like hydrogen bonding. The strong force also lead to close packing of chain.
solution_html: (i) and (iv) is correct options because nylon and polyester are thread forming fibers which possesses high tensile strength and high melting point. They have strong intermolecular forces like hydrogen bonding. The strong force also lead to close packing of chain.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Vulcanisation makes rubber ________. more elastic soluble in inorganic solvent crystalline more stiff
question_html:
Vulcanisation makes rubber ________.

  1. more elastic
  2. soluble in inorganic solvent
  3. crystalline
  4. more stiff
solution_text: Vulcanization is done to improve the physical properties of rubber it makes rubber more elastic and stiffer. On vulcanization sulphur forms cross-links at the reactive sites of double bonds or at their reactive allylic position and thus the rubber gets stiffened. Therefore, (i) and (iv) is correct.
solution_html: Vulcanization is done to improve the physical properties of rubber it makes rubber more elastic and stiffer. On vulcanization sulphur forms cross-links at the reactive sites of double bonds or at their reactive allylic position and thus the rubber gets stiffened. Therefore, (i) and (iv) is correct.

question_html: A natural linear polymer of 2-methyl-1, 3-butadiene becomes hard on treatment with sulphur between 373 to 415 K and -S-S- bonds are formed between chains. Write the structure of the product of this treatment?

When it is heated with s at 373-415 K, it results in -S-S- links forming.

question_html:

-A - A- A - A - A -.

-A-B-B-A-A-A-B-A-
solution_text: -A-B-B-A-A-A-B-A- is a copolymer, since, its repeating structural unit is derived from two types of monomer units, i.e., A and B.
solution_html: -A-B-B-A-A-A-B-A- is a copolymer, since, its repeating structural unit is derived from two types of monomer units, i.e., A and B.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why are rubbers called elastomers?
question_html: Why are rubbers called elastomers?
solution_text: Rubbers are stretched on the application of force and come to its original position after the force is removed(i.e; regain its original shape). Therefore, these are called elastomers.
solution_html: Rubbers are stretched on the application of force and come to its original position after the force is removed(i.e; regain its original shape). Therefore, these are called elastomers.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Can enzyme be called a polymer?
question_html: Can enzyme be called a polymer?
solution_text: Yes, enzymes are biocatalysts which are protein and thus polymers.
solution_html: Yes, enzymes are biocatalysts which are protein and thus polymers.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Can nucleic acids, proteins, and starch be considered as step growth polymers?
question_html: Can nucleic acids, proteins, and starch be considered as step growth polymers?
solution_text: Yes, step-growth polymers (nucleic acids, proteins, and starch) are condensation polymer and they can be formed by the loss of simple molecules like water leading to the formation of high molecular mass polymers process is known as step-growth polymerisation.
solution_html: Yes, step-growth polymers (nucleic acids, proteins, and starch) are condensation polymer and they can be formed by the loss of simple molecules like water leading to the formation of high molecular mass polymers process is known as step-growth polymerisation.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: How is the following resin intermediate prepared and which polymer is formed by this monomer unit?
question_html: How is the following resin intermediate prepared and which polymer is formed by this monomer unit?
solution_text: The melamine and formaldehyde are starting materials for this intermediate. Its condensation polymerization gives melamine-formaldehyde polymer.
solution_html: The melamine and formaldehyde are starting materials for this intermediate. Its condensation polymerization gives melamine-formaldehyde polymer.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: To have practical applications why are cross-links required in rubber?
question_html: To have practical applications why are cross-links required in rubber?
solution_text: The cross-links make the rubber hard, tough with greater tensile strength. The vulcanized rubber has excellent elasticity, low water absorption tendency, resistance to oxidation arid organic solvents. Cross-links help the rubber to stretch.
solution_html: The cross-links make the rubber hard, tough with greater tensile strength. The vulcanized rubber has excellent elasticity, low water absorption tendency, resistance to oxidation arid organic solvents. Cross-links help the rubber to stretch.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why does cis-polyisoprene possess elastic property?
question_html: Why does cis-polyisoprene possess elastic property?
solution_text: The cis-polyisoprene (natural rubber) molecule consists of various chains held together by weak van der Waals interactions and has a coiled structure. Thus, it can be stretched like a spring and shows elastic properties.
solution_html: The cis-polyisoprene (natural rubber) molecule consists of various chains held together by weak van der Waals interactions and has a coiled structure. Thus, it can be stretched like a spring and shows elastic properties.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What is the structural difference between HDP and LDP? How does the structure account for different behavior and nature, hence the use of a polymer?
question_html: What is the structural difference between HDP and LDP? How does the structure account for different behavior and nature, hence the use of a polymer?
solution_text: LDP (low - density polythene) HDP (high - density polythene) It consists of a highly branched chain molecule. It consists of a linear chain molecule. Due to branching, molecules do not pack well. The molecule is closely packed in space. It has a low density (0.93gcm 3 ) and has a low melting point. It has a high density (0.97gcm 3 ) and has a high melting point. LDP- is transparent of moderate tensile strength and high toughness( use in wash bottles). HDP is quite hard, tougher ( Use in dustbin, bottle, pipes), and has high tensile strength.
solution_html:
LDP (low-density polythene) HDP (high-density polythene)
It consists of a highly branched chain molecule.

It consists of a linear chain molecule.
Due to branching, molecules do not pack well. The molecule is closely packed in space.
It has a low density (0.93gcm3) and has a low melting point. It has a high density (0.97gcm3) and has a high melting point.
LDP- is transparent of moderate tensile strength and high toughness( use in wash bottles).

HDP is quite hard, tougher ( Use in dustbin, bottle, pipes), and has high tensile strength.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What is the role of benzoyl peroxide in the addition polymerisation of alkenes? Explain its mode of action with the help of an example.
question_html: What is the role of benzoyl peroxide in the addition polymerisation of alkenes? Explain its mode of action with the help of an example.
solution_text: Benzoyl peroxide acts as an initiator in free radical addition polymerization of alkenes by providing chain initiation. The radical formed adds to the carbon-carbon double bond of alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer. The new free radical adds to a double bond of monomer to form a larger free radical which adds to another alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer this step is called the chain initiating step. {tex}{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{||} }\limits_O - {\text{O}} - {\text{O}} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{||} }\limits_O - {{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{Heat}}{/tex} {tex}2{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{||} }\limits_O - \mathop {\text{O}}\limits^. \to \mathop {\mathop {2{{\text{C}}_6}{{\mathop {\text{H}}\limits^. }_5}}\limits_{Phenyl} }\limits_{radical} + 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}{/tex} The radical formed adds to the carbon-carbon double bond of alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer. The new free radical adds to a double bond of monomer to form a larger free radical which adds to other alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer.
solution_html:
Benzoyl peroxide acts as an initiator in free radical addition polymerization of alkenes by providing chain initiation. The radical formed adds to the carbon-carbon double bond of alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer. The new free radical adds to a double bond of monomer to form a larger free radical which adds to another alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer this step is called the chain initiating step.
{tex}{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{||} }\limits_O - {\text{O}} - {\text{O}} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{||} }\limits_O - {{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{/tex}  {tex}\xrightarrow{{Heat}}{/tex}  {tex}2{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{||} }\limits_O - \mathop {\text{O}}\limits^. \to \mathop {\mathop {2{{\text{C}}_6}{{\mathop {\text{H}}\limits^. }_5}}\limits_{Phenyl} }\limits_{radical} + 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}{/tex}
The radical formed adds to the carbon-carbon double bond of alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer.

The new free radical adds to a double bond of monomer to form a larger free radical which adds to other alkene molecules and ultimately forms the polymer.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which factor imparts crystalline nature to a polymer like nylon?
question_html: Which factor imparts crystalline nature to a polymer like nylon?
solution_text: Fibers have strong intermolecular forces like hydrogen bonding and linear structure lead to close packing of polymer chains that impart crystalline character.
solution_html: Fibers have strong intermolecular forces like hydrogen bonding and linear structure lead to close packing of polymer chains that impart crystalline character.

Urea: NH2CONH2
Formaldehyde: HCHO

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which type of biomolecules have some structural similarity with synthetic polyamides? What is this similarity?
question_html: Which type of biomolecules have some structural similarity with synthetic polyamides? What is this similarity?
solution_text: Proteins have structural similarities with synthetic polyamides. Polyamides and proteins both contain amide linkage(N-acylation).
solution_html: Proteins have structural similarities with synthetic polyamides. Polyamides and proteins both contain amide linkage(N-acylation).

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why should the monomers used in addition polymerisation through free radical pathway be very pure?
question_html: Why should the monomers used in addition polymerisation through free radical pathway be very pure?
solution_text: Pure monomers are required because even the traces of impurities may act like inhibitors which leads to the formation of polymers with shorter chain length.
solution_html: Pure monomers are required because even the traces of impurities may act like inhibitors which leads to the formation of polymers with shorter chain length.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Match the polymer of column I with correct monomer of column II. Column I Column II (a) High density polythene (i) Isoprene (b) Neoprene (ii) Tetrafluoroethene (c) Natural rubber (iii) Chloroprene (d) Teflon (iv) Acrylonitrile (e) Acrilan (v) Ethene
question_html:
Match the polymer of column I with correct monomer of column II.

Column I Column II
(a) High density polythene (i) Isoprene
(b) Neoprene (ii) Tetrafluoroethene
(c) Natural rubber (iii) Chloroprene
(d) Teflon (iv) Acrylonitrile
(e) Acrilan (v) Ethene
solution_text: (a) - (v), (b) - (iii), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii), (e) - (iv) Neoprene is formed by the free radicals polymerization of chloroprene. High density polyethylene formed when addition polymerization of ethene take place.
solution_html:
(a) - (v), (b) - (iii), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii), (e) - (iv)

Neoprene is formed by the free radicals polymerization of chloroprene.
High density polyethylene formed when addition polymerization of ethene take place.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Match the polymers given in Column I with their commercial names given in Column II Column I Column II (a) Polyester of glycol and phthalic acid (i) Novolac (b) Copolymer of 1,3-butadiene and styrene (ii) Glyptal (c) Phenol and formaldehyde resin (iii) Buna-S (d) Polyester of glycol and terephthalic acid (iv) Buna-N (e) Copolymer of 1, 3-butadiene and acrylonitrile (v) Dacron
question_html:
Match the polymers given in Column I with their commercial names given in Column II

Column I Column II
(a) Polyester of glycol and phthalic acid (i) Novolac
(b) Copolymer of 1,3-butadiene and styrene (ii) Glyptal
(c) Phenol and formaldehyde resin (iii) Buna-S
(d) Polyester of glycol and terephthalic acid (iv) Buna-N
(e) Copolymer of 1, 3-butadiene and acrylonitrile (v) Dacron
solution_text: Correct matching sequence- (a) - (ii), (b) - (iii), (c) - (i), (d) - (v), (e) - (iv)

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Synthetic polymers do not degrade in the environment for a long time. How can biodegradable synthetic polymers be made? Differentiate between biopolymers and biodegradable polymers and give examples of each type.
question_html: Synthetic polymers do not degrade in the environment for a long time. How can biodegradable synthetic polymers be made? Differentiate between biopolymers and biodegradable polymers and give examples of each type.
solution_text: Synthetic polymers are quite resistant to the environmental degradation process and are responsible for the accumulation of polymer solid waste material. This solid waste cause acute environmental problem. New biodegradable synthetic polymers have been designed and developed. These polymers contain similar functional groups as present in biopolymers, e.g., aliphatic polyesters Biopolymers are polymers of amino acids or carbohydrates which are linked to each other by peptide or glycosidic linkages as shown below: The polymer of amino acid Biopolymer can or cannot be biodegradable. e.g., protein, starch, etc., are biodegradable but keratin is non-biodegradable. The biodegradable polymer is polymers that can be degradable always, e.g., DHBV nylon-2, nylon-6.
solution_html:
Synthetic polymers are quite resistant to the environmental degradation process and are responsible for the accumulation of polymer solid waste material. This solid waste cause acute environmental problem. New biodegradable synthetic polymers have been designed and developed. These polymers contain similar functional groups as present in biopolymers, e.g., aliphatic polyesters Biopolymers are polymers of amino acids or carbohydrates which are linked to each other by peptide or glycosidic linkages as shown below:


The polymer of amino acid Biopolymer can or cannot be biodegradable. e.g., protein, starch, etc., are biodegradable but keratin is non-biodegradable. The biodegradable polymer is polymers that can be degradable always, e.g., DHBV nylon-2, nylon-6.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate between rubbers and plastics on the basis of intermolecular forces.
question_html: Differentiate between rubbers and plastics on the basis of intermolecular forces.
solution_text: Rubber is a natural polymer and possesses elastic properties that are capable of returning to its original length, shape, or size after being stretched or deformed. It is a common example of an elastomer The m-polyisoprene molecule (natural rubber) consists of various chains held together by weak Van Der Waals interactions and has a coiled structure. Thus, It can be stretch like spring. Thermoplastics are the polymers in which intermolecular forces of attraction are in between those of elastomers and fibers. They are neither very strong nor very weak and have no cross-links between the chains molecules capable of repeatedly softening on heating and hardening on cooling.
solution_html: Rubber is a natural polymer and possesses elastic properties that are capable of returning to its original length, shape, or size after being stretched or deformed. It is a common example of an elastomer The m-polyisoprene molecule (natural rubber) consists of various chains held together by weak Van Der Waals interactions and has a coiled structure.  Thus, It can be stretch like spring. Thermoplastics are the polymers in which intermolecular forces of attraction are in between those of elastomers and fibers. They are neither very strong nor very weak and have no cross-links between the chains molecules capable of repeatedly softening on heating and hardening on cooling.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Phenol and formaldehyde undergo condensation to give a polymer (A) which on heating with formaldehyde gives a thermosetting polymer (B). Name the polymers. Write the reactions involved in the formation of (A). What is the structural difference between two polymers?
question_html: Phenol and formaldehyde undergo condensation to give a polymer (A) which on heating with formaldehyde gives a thermosetting polymer (B). Name the polymers. Write the reactions involved in the formation of (A). What is the structural difference between two polymers?
solution_text: Polymer 'A' is Novalac and "B' is Bakelite. The reactions involved in the formation of these two polymers are given below: Novalac on heating with formaldehyde HCHO undergoes cross-linkage to form an infusible solid mass called Bakelite. Novolac is a linear chain polymer while Bakelite is a cross-linked polymer used in combs.
solution_html:
Polymer 'A' is Novalac and "B' is Bakelite. The reactions involved in the formation of these two polymers are given below:


Novalac on heating with formaldehyde HCHO undergoes cross-linkage to form an infusible solid mass called Bakelite. Novolac is a linear chain polymer while Bakelite is a cross-linked polymer used in combs.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Low-density polythene and high-density polythene, both are polymers of ethene but there is marked difference in their properties. Explain.
question_html: Low-density polythene and high-density polythene, both are polymers of ethene but there is marked difference in their properties. Explain.
solution_text: LDP -( low - density polythene ) HDP ( high - density polythene ) LDP- is obtained through polymerization of ethene at high pressure (1000 to 2000atm) and a temperature of 350K to 570K and is a highly branched structure. HDP is obtained by addition polymerization of ethene take place in the presence of a hydrocarbon solvent at low pressure (6-7 atm) and a temperature of 333K - 343K in the presence of Ziegler- Natta catalysts such as trimethylaluminium and titanium tetrachloride. Under these conditions, the polymer is linear and has a high density due to close packing. LDP- is chemically inert and tough but slightly flexible. It is tougher and harder than LDP.
solution_html:
LDP-(low-density polythene)       HDP(high-density polythene)
LDP- is obtained through polymerization of ethene at high pressure (1000 to 2000atm) and a temperature of 350K to 570K and is a highly branched structure. HDP is obtained by addition polymerization of ethene take place in the presence of a hydrocarbon solvent at low pressure (6-7 atm) and a temperature of 333K - 343K in the presence of Ziegler- Natta catalysts such as trimethylaluminium and titanium tetrachloride. Under these conditions, the polymer is linear and has a high density due to close packing.
LDP- is chemically inert and tough but slightly flexible. It is tougher and harder than LDP.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following polymers soften on heating and harden on cooling? What are the polymers with this property collectively called? What are the structural similarities between such polymers? Bakelite, urea-formaldehyde resin, polythene, polyvinyls, polystyrene.
question_html:
Which of the following polymers soften on heating and harden on cooling? What are the polymers with this property collectively called? What are the structural similarities between such polymers?
Bakelite, urea-formaldehyde resin, polythene, polyvinyls, polystyrene.
solution_text: Polythene, polyvinyls and polystyrene soften on heating and harden on cooling. Such polymers are called thermoplastic polymers. These polymers are linear or slightly branched long chain molecules. They possess intermolecular forces whose strength lies between strength of intermolecular forces of elastomers and fibres.
solution_html: Polythene, polyvinyls and polystyrene soften on heating and harden on cooling. Such polymers are called thermoplastic polymers. These polymers are linear or slightly branched long chain molecules. They possess intermolecular forces whose strength lies between strength of intermolecular forces of elastomers and fibres.
